cards.grammarNote: Pure Korean numbers are used with age (살), hours (시), people (명), and general counters (개). These are the everyday counting numbers.
열 (10) is the base for teens in Pure Korean: 열하나 (11), 열둘 (12), etc. 스물 = 20, 서른 = 30.
Sino-Korean numbers are used with minutes (분), dates, money (원), floors (층), phone numbers, and years.
십 (10) is the base for Sino-Korean tens: 십일 (11), 이십 (20), 삼십 (30), 백 (100), 천 (1,000).

cards.front: 한, 두, 세, 네 — when do these forms appear?
cards.back: 하나→한, 둘→두, 셋→세, 넷→네 before a counter noun
cards.grammarNote: Pure Korean 1–4 drop their final sound before a counter: 한 명 (one person), 두 개 (two items), 세 살 (three years old). Never say 하나 명 or 둘 개.

cards.front: How old are you? / I'm 25 years old.
cards.grammarNote: Age uses Pure Korean numbers + 살 (sal). 스물 = 20, 다섯 = 5, so 스물다섯 = 25. Note: 한 살 (1 year old) uses the shortened form 한, not 하나.

cards.front: What time is it? / It's 2 o'clock.
cards.grammarNote: Hours use Pure Korean numbers + 시 (si). 두 시 = 2 o'clock (two [pure] + hour). Note the shortened form: 두, not 둘.
Minutes use Sino-Korean numbers + 분 (bun). 십 (10) + 분 = 십분 (10 minutes). 후에 = after/later. Contrast with hours which use Pure Korean.
Floors use Sino-Korean numbers + 층 (cheung). 삼 (3) + 층 = 3층. Also applies to building numbers, apartment floors, etc.
Telling time mixes both systems: hours in Pure Korean (두 시 = 2 o'clock), minutes in Sino-Korean (삼십 분 = 30 minutes). This mixed pattern is fixed — you cannot swap the systems.

cards.front: Which number system for prices in Korean won (원)?
cards.grammarNote: Money uses Sino-Korean: 오천 원 (5,000 won), 만 원 (10,000 won). 천 = 1,000, 만 = 10,000. Large numbers are built on units of 만 (10,000), not 1,000 as in English.
People use Pure Korean numbers + 명 (myeong). 세 is the shortened form of 셋 used before a counter. Formal/official contexts may use 분 (bun) as a polite counter for people.
